narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 55.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision to reject your application—they use this threshold to prioritize students likely to succeed right away. Your profile shows strong alignment with many typical applicants: as a white female planning full-time studies from an upper-middle-income family, you're right in line with the majority of students in the dataset, where whites make up about 84%, females around 44%, full-timers over 92%, and that income level is average. However, your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 is just a bit below the typical 3.21, which didn't help much. What stood out most to the model was your LSAT score of 27, well below the average of 36.7—this was the top factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower scores like yours signal higher risk compared to peers who passed the bar. Next in influence came your Year 3 school decile of 1, far below the average 5.53, suggesting your later academic performance ranked very low among classmates and nudged the model further toward expecting bar struggles. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also much lower than the typical 5.71, ranked third in impact and reinforced that early pattern of underperforming relative to others. Family income played a moderate role after that, with your upper-middle bracket not shifting things much since it's so common. Your undergraduate GPA followed, slightly dragging the odds as it fell short of the norm, while your race and gender had even smaller effects, staying neutral or minimally toward failure given their prevalence in successful students. Overall, these academic markers—especially the LSAT and low school deciles—dominated the decision, outweighing your demographic fits with typical bar-passers. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT aiming for a score closer to 37 or higher through targeted practice, as that's the most influential factor and fully changeable. Building stronger study habits now could mimic the trajectories of top decile students who passed the bar, perhaps by seeking tutoring or prep courses to boost performance metrics. With those steps, you'd stand out more favorably next time—keep pushing, you've got this potential.
